stem: The antiderivative \( F(x) \) of \( f(x) = [[c]]x^{[[n]]} \) satisfies \( F(0) = 0 \). What is \( F([[b]]) \)?
solution: By the power rule for antiderivatives, \( F(x) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}x^{[[n]]+1} \) (using \( F(0)=0 \) to fix the constant), so \( F([[b]]) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}\cdot [[b]]^{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f([[b]]) \) directly instead of integrating to get \( F([[b]]) \).
This forgets to divide by \( [[n]]+1 \) after raising the power.
This divides by \( [[n]] \) instead of \( [[n]]+1 \) in the power rule.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[c]] \) from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.

stem: An antiderivative \( F(x) \) of \( f(x) = [[p]]x^{2} + ([[q]])x + ([[r]]) \) satisfies \( F([[a]]) = 0 \). What is \( F([[b]]) \)?
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( F([[b]]) - F([[a]]) = \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left([[p]]x^{2}+([[q]])x+([[r]])\right)dx \). Since \( F([[a]])=0 \), this equals \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{3}\left([[b]]^{3}-[[a]]^{3}\right) + \dfrac{[[q]]}{2}\left([[b]]^{2}-[[a]]^{2}\right) + [[r]]\left([[b]]-[[a]]\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f([[b]]) \) directly instead of integrating over \( [[a]],[[b]] \).
This uses a right-endpoint rectangle approximation instead of the exact antiderivative.
This uses the wrong denominator (2 instead of 3) for the cubic power-rule term.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.

stem: The antiderivative \( F(x) \) of \( f(x) = [[c]]\sin(x) \) satisfies \( F(0) = 0 \). What is \( F\!\left(\dfrac{[[j]]\pi}{3}\right) \)?
solution: Since \( \int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x + C \) and \( F(0)=0 \) fixes the constant, \( F(x) = [[c]]\left(1-\cos x\right) \). So \( F\!\left(\tfrac{[[j]]\pi}{3}\right) = [[c]]\left(1-\cos\!\left(\tfrac{[[j]]\pi}{3}\right)\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( [[c]]\cos\!\left(\tfrac{[[j]]\pi}{3}\right) \) alone, forgetting the \( 1-\cos(\cdot) \) structure from \( F(0)=0 \).
This flips the sign of the cosine term but still forgets to subtract it from 1.
This evaluates \( f\!\left(\tfrac{[[j]]\pi}{3}\right) \) directly instead of integrating to get \( F \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[c]] \) from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.

solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( F([[b]]) = F([[a]]) + \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left([[m]]x+([[k]])\right)dx = [[C]] + \dfrac{[[m]]}{2}\left([[b]]^{2}-[[a]]^{2}\right) + [[k]]\left([[b]]-[[a]]\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes only the net change and forgets to add the initial value \( F([[a]])=[[C]] \).
This wrongly applies the power-rule antiderivative as if \( F(0)=[[C]] \) rather than \( F([[a]])=[[C]] \).
This uses the rate \( F'([[b]]) \) itself instead of integrating it.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[m]] \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( [[m]] \) from the correct value in the other direction.

stem: The general antiderivative of \( f(x) = [[c]]x^{[[n]]} \) can be written as \( F(x) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1}x^{[[n]]+1} + C \). If \( F([[x0]]) = [[k]] \), what is the value of \( C \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds the power term instead of subtracting it, a sign error when isolating \( C \).
This uses the wrong power (\( [[n]] \) instead of \( [[n]]+1 \)) and forgets to divide.
This reports just the power term itself, forgetting to subtract it from \( [[k]] \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value in the other direction.
